title: Te Whakapapa o te Hauora: The Living Legacy of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the healing traditions of Ngāti Kahungunu along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Stream of the Bellbird). Students will explore how their ancestors used the stream's resources for rongoā (traditional medicine), physical healing, and spiritual wellbeing, connecting to stories of tohunga rongoā who once practiced along these banks.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional rongoā practices connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and explain their health benefits
Create a personal hauora plan incorporating one traditional Māori wellbeing practice and one modern health strategy
Demonstrate proper tikanga and kaitiakitanga when gathering and preparing natural resources from the local environment
Compare and contrast traditional Māori and contemporary Western approaches to holistic wellbeing in a short reflective piece

activities.title: Whakatau: Karakia and Connection to the Awa
activities.description: Begin with karakia to open the learning space safely. Students will sit beside Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, observing and journaling about what the waterway provides for physical, mental, and spiritual wellbeing through a Māori lens.

activities.title: Mahi Rongoā: Traditional Medicine Practices
activities.description: Working with local kaumatua (Aunty Hine Palmer), students will learn to identify kawakawa, harakeke, and other medicinal plants growing near the awa. They will prepare simple rongoā such as kawakawa balm or harakeke dressings, following proper protocols.

activities.title: Waiata Hauora: Healing Through Song and Movement
activities.description: Learn traditional waiata and haka that were used for physical conditioning, emotional expression, and community bonding. Focus on waiata specific to Ngāti Kahungunu that connect to water and healing.

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Stream Health Assessment
activities.description: Conduct water quality testing and environmental assessment of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, documenting how the health of the waterway directly impacts the health of the community that depends on it.

activities.title: Whakawhiti Kōrero: Intergenerational Knowledge Sharing
activities.description: Students interview kaumātua and community members about traditional health practices and present their findings through digital storytelling, connecting historical practices to modern health challenges.

resources: Aunty Hine Palmer (local Ngāti Kahungunu rongoā expert)
'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku water testing kits from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
Digital recording equipment for interviewing community members

tasks: Create a digital portfolio documenting the rongoā preparation process with reflections
Develop and present a personal hauora plan incorporating traditional practices
Write a comparative analysis of traditional and modern approaches to health
Demonstrate proper tikanga during practical activities through teacher observation

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 Personal Health and Physical Development
Health and Physical Education: C3 Relationships with Other People
Health and Physical Education: D4 Healthy Communities and Environments
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
